title: Relevant Costs and Decision Making
description: Test your understanding of relevant costs, irrelevant costs, sunk costs, opportunity costs and managerial decision making.

questions.question: What is a relevant cost?
options: A future cost that differs between decision alternatives
Any cost recorded in the accounting system
A cost that has already been incurred
A cost that never changes
questions.explanation: Relevant costs are future costs that differ between alternatives and therefore influence the decision.

questions.question: Which type of cost should generally be ignored when making business decisions?
options: Relevant costs
Opportunity costs
Sunk costs
Incremental costs
questions.explanation: Sunk costs have already been incurred and cannot be changed by any future decision.

questions.question: What is an opportunity cost?
options: The total manufacturing cost of a product
The benefit given up by choosing one alternative over another
A fixed operating expense
The depreciation of equipment
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost represents the benefit sacrificed when one alternative is chosen instead of another.

questions.question: A company has already spent $200,000 developing a new product. Management must now decide whether to launch it. How should the development cost be treated?
options: As a relevant cost
As a sunk cost
As an opportunity cost
As an incremental revenue
questions.explanation: The development cost has already been incurred and cannot be recovered, making it a sunk cost.

questions.question: Which statement best describes an irrelevant cost?
options: It changes depending on the decision
It remains the same regardless of the decision
It is always a variable cost
It only exists in manufacturing companies
questions.explanation: Irrelevant costs do not differ between alternatives and therefore should not influence the decision.

questions.question: What are incremental costs?
options: Costs that have already been paid
Additional costs resulting from choosing one alternative
Allocated overhead costs
Fixed costs that never change
questions.explanation: Incremental costs are additional costs that occur because of a specific decision.

questions.question: A factory pays $30,000 per month in rent regardless of production volume. When evaluating whether to accept a one-time special order, how is the rent normally classified?
options: Relevant cost
Opportunity cost
Irrelevant cost
Incremental cost
questions.explanation: Since the rent will be paid regardless of the decision, it is irrelevant for this particular analysis.

questions.question: Why are opportunity costs important even though they usually do not appear in accounting records?
options: They represent taxes paid on future profits
They measure the value of benefits forgone by choosing one alternative
They eliminate fixed costs
They replace contribution margin calculations
questions.explanation: Opportunity costs capture the value of the best alternative that is sacrificed.

questions.question: A company has unused production capacity and receives a special order. Which costs are usually most relevant when evaluating the order?
options: Additional variable production costs
Historical depreciation
Past marketing expenses
Allocated fixed costs that will not change
questions.explanation: When unused capacity exists, the additional variable costs are normally the primary relevant costs.

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes the purpose of relevant cost analysis?
options: To include every accounting cost in the decision
To focus only on costs and revenues that change between alternatives
To eliminate uncertainty from business decisions
To maximize sales regardless of profitability
questions.explanation: Relevant cost analysis helps managers ignore information that will not affect the outcome of the decision.

questions.question: A company must choose between producing a component internally or purchasing it from an external supplier. Which costs are most relevant to this decision?
options: Avoidable manufacturing costs and the supplier's purchase price
Historical research and development costs
Allocated corporate overhead that will remain unchanged
The original purchase price of the production equipment
questions.explanation: Managers should compare the costs that differ between the two alternatives, including avoidable production costs and the supplier's price.

questions.question: A supplier offers to sell a component for $18 per unit. Internal production costs $20 per unit, but $5 consists of unavoidable fixed costs. Which alternative is financially preferable?
options: Purchase from the supplier
Continue producing internally
Both alternatives cost exactly the same
There is not enough information
questions.explanation: The relevant internal production cost is only $15 ($20 − $5 unavoidable fixed costs), making internal production cheaper than buying for $18.

questions.question: A manager is deciding whether to discontinue a product line that appears unprofitable. Which cost should be examined most carefully?
options: Avoidable fixed costs
Historical depreciation
Sunk costs
Allocated fixed costs that will remain regardless
questions.explanation: Only fixed costs that can actually be avoided by discontinuing the product are relevant.

questions.question: Why can allocating fixed costs across products sometimes lead to poor decisions?
options: Because allocated costs may continue even if a product is discontinued
Because allocated costs are always variable
Because accounting standards prohibit allocations
Because contribution margin becomes irrelevant
questions.explanation: Many allocated fixed costs remain after a product is discontinued, making them irrelevant for that decision.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a make-or-buy decision?
options: A decision about whether to manufacture a product internally or purchase it externally
A decision about increasing selling prices
A decision about calculating depreciation
A decision about allocating indirect costs
questions.explanation: Make-or-buy decisions compare the costs and benefits of internal production versus outsourcing.

questions.question: A company has already invested $2 million in machinery. The equipment is now outdated, and replacing it would reduce future operating costs. What should management focus on?
options: Future cost savings from replacing the equipment
Recovering the original investment
The historical purchase price of the machinery
Ignoring future operating costs
questions.explanation: The original investment is a sunk cost. Managers should focus on future costs and benefits.

questions.question: A business has enough production capacity to manufacture only one of two products. Which concept becomes especially important?
options: Opportunity cost
Depreciation
Historical cost
Allocated overhead
questions.explanation: Choosing one product means giving up the profit from the other, creating an opportunity cost.

questions.question: Why should managers include qualitative factors when making decisions?
options: Because financial analysis does not capture factors such as quality, customer satisfaction and supplier reliability
Because qualitative factors always have a larger financial impact
Because accounting data is unnecessary
Because opportunity costs become irrelevant
questions.explanation: Some important business considerations cannot easily be measured in monetary terms but may still determine the best decision.

questions.question: Two suppliers offer nearly identical prices. One has a history of frequent delivery delays. Which decision is generally best?
options: Consider supplier reliability alongside the financial comparison
Always choose the cheaper supplier regardless of service
Ignore delivery performance because it is qualitative
Choose the supplier with the oldest contract
questions.explanation: Reliable deliveries may reduce operational disruptions, making a slightly more expensive supplier the better choice.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why relevant cost analysis is useful?
options: It helps managers focus only on information that will change depending on the decision
It guarantees the correct decision in every situation
It eliminates business risk
It replaces strategic planning
questions.explanation: Relevant cost analysis filters out unnecessary information so managers can compare alternatives more effectively.

questions.question: A company has already spent $750,000 developing a new product. Market research now suggests future demand will be much lower than expected. What should management do?
options: Base the decision on future revenues and future relevant costs
Launch the product because the development cost was so high
Ignore future market conditions because the money has already been spent
Continue the project until the development cost has been recovered
questions.explanation: The development cost is a sunk cost. Management should base its decision on future costs, future revenues and expected profitability.

questions.question: A factory is operating at full capacity. Producing Product A means Product B cannot be produced. Which concept is most important when evaluating the decision?
options: Opportunity cost
Depreciation expense
Historical cost
Sunk cost
questions.explanation: When capacity is limited, choosing one product means sacrificing the contribution from another product.

questions.question: A supplier offers slightly lower prices, but its deliveries are often delayed, causing production interruptions. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Evaluate both financial and qualitative factors before deciding
Always choose the cheapest supplier
Ignore delivery performance because it is difficult to measure
Select the supplier with the lowest historical prices
questions.explanation: Reliable deliveries may outweigh a small price advantage because production interruptions can be very costly.

questions.question: A company is deciding whether to discontinue a product that shows an accounting loss. Most allocated fixed costs will remain after the product is removed. What is the best conclusion?
options: The product should only be discontinued if the avoidable costs exceed the contribution it generates
The product should always be discontinued because it reports an accounting loss
Allocated fixed costs disappear immediately after discontinuation
The accounting profit alone determines the correct decision
questions.explanation: Managers should focus on avoidable costs and contribution margin rather than allocated accounting costs.

questions.question: A manufacturer receives a one-time order at a lower selling price. The factory has significant unused capacity, and the order covers all additional variable costs while generating extra contribution margin. What is generally the best decision?
options: Accept the order if it does not negatively affect existing business
Reject the order because the selling price is below the normal price
Reject the order because fixed costs have already been paid
Accept the order only if it increases the selling price of future orders
questions.explanation: With unused capacity, any positive contribution margin generally increases operating profit, provided there are no negative strategic consequences.

questions.question: A shipping company can either accept a charter today or keep the vessel available in the hope of securing a higher-paying contract next week. Which concept best captures the potential cost of accepting today's charter?
options: Opportunity cost
Sunk cost
Depreciation expense
Historical cost
questions.explanation: Accepting today's charter may mean giving up the profit from a more profitable future charter, creating an opportunity cost.

questions.question: A business manager argues that a poor investment should continue because 'we have already spent too much money to stop now.' Which decision-making error is being made?
options: Allowing sunk costs to influence future decisions
Ignoring contribution margin
Using incremental analysis
Applying target pricing
questions.explanation: Past expenditures cannot be recovered and should never determine future decisions.

questions.question: Why is relevant cost analysis considered a better decision-making tool than relying solely on financial statements?
options: Because it focuses only on costs and revenues that will change between alternatives
Because it eliminates uncertainty from business decisions
Because financial statements are inaccurate
Because it guarantees higher profits
questions.explanation: Financial statements include many historical and allocated costs that may not be relevant to a specific decision.

questions.question: Which statement best describes how experienced managers make important business decisions?
options: They combine relevant cost analysis with qualitative and strategic considerations
They always choose the option with the lowest accounting cost
They focus primarily on sunk costs to recover previous investments
They ignore uncertainty and rely only on financial calculations
questions.explanation: Good decisions require both quantitative analysis and consideration of strategic, operational and qualitative factors.

questions.question: What is the most important principle of relevant cost analysis?
options: Only future costs and benefits that differ between alternatives should influence a decision
Every accounting cost should be included in every decision
Historical costs are the most reliable basis for future decisions
Fixed costs are always relevant regardless of the situation
questions.explanation: The core principle of relevant cost analysis is to focus exclusively on future costs and revenues that change depending on the decision.
